'''Shaun Ainscough''' (born 27 November 1989<ref name="WWFProfile">{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Player&=217513|title=Shaun Ainscough|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref>) is a former English professional [[rugby league]] footballer who last played as a {{rlp|wg}}er for the [[Rochdale Hornets]] in the [[RFL Championship|Championship]].

Ainscough started his professional career for the [[Wigan Warriors]] in the [[Super League]], and has also played for the [[Bradford Bulls]]. He now works at CM Health and Fitness personal training when not playing rugby league.

He caused a sensation by scoring sixteen tries in his first ten professional games, becoming the league's leading try scorer at one point.

==Background==
Ainscough was born in [[Billinge Higher End]], [[Metropolitan Borough of Wigan|Wigan]], England.<ref name="WWFProfile"/>

==Early life==
Ainscough's first experience of competitive rugby league football came through Wigan St Judes ARLFC.<ref name="OfficialProfile">{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/SquadMember.asp?id=221&teamid=1|title=Shaun Ainscough|publisher=www.wiganwarriors.com ([[Wigan Warriors]])|accessdate=2009-06-30}} {{Dead link|date=October 2010|bot=H3llBot}}</ref>

He remained at the amateur club until 2006, when he gained a scholarship to join the academy of his hometown professional club, [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]].<ref name="WWFProfile" /> His first game for the academy came in a Junior Academy Championship fixture at [[Hilton Park (stadium)|Hilton Park]] against [[Leigh Centurions|Leigh]], as a {{rlp|wg}}]. Ainscough marked this appearance by also scoring his first try in a Wigan jersey.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=177418|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Leigh 6, Wigan 64|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> He was rotated into different positions throughout his academy career, playing at centre for a repeat match against Leigh seven weeks later, as well as fullback against [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]] the game after that. On both occasions, Ainscough scored tries, earning one and two tries in each game respectively.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=177720|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Leigh 12, Wigan 58|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=177763|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Harlequins RL 4, Wigan 48|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> However, his first competitive [[hat-trick]] came playing on the left wing in a play-off game against [[Widnes Vikings|Widnes]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=374869|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Wigan 42, Widnes 6|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> During his time with the academy in 2007, Ainscough also played two matches at senior level, once against [[Hull F.C.|Hull FC]], and once against Harlequins RL, scoring a try in the latter fixture.<ref>
{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=280656|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Wigan 36, Hull 28|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=280659|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Harlequins RL 24, Wigan 42|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref>

His energetic style on the wing was noted by the club's coaches, and this continued to impress officials inside the club and out, leading to him earning a place for the England under-17s team during the [[Australian Institute of Sport]]'s tour of the United Kingdom and France in 2007.<ref>
{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/news_full.asp?newsid=3429|title=Four Warriors Named for England Academy|publisher=www.wiganwarriors.com (Wigan Warriors)|accessdate=2009-06-30}} {{Dead link|date=October 2010|bot=H3llBot}}</ref> The second test of this series ended with a victory for the England team winning 38–22, thanks in no small part to a hat-trick of tries from Ainscough, and another from [[Richard Myler]], who would also go on to prominence in the Super League with fellow [[Lancashire|Lancastrians]], [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]].<ref name="U17">{{cite web|url=http://www.ourfootyteam.com/ais_2006Tour.php|title=Victory for England Academy|publisher=www.ourfootyteam.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref>

Although he was not yet officially a part of Wigan first team, his debut appearance for the club was in a friendly match against Bradford Bulls in January 2008, wearing jersey number 31. It was a successful start for the then-eighteen-year-old, scoring two of Wigan's five tries, each of them either side of the half-time break in a match Wigan won 16–28.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://wigan.rlfans.com/print.php?type=N&item_id=523|title=Match Details: Bradford 16 Wigan 28|publisher=wigan.rlfans.com (cherryandwhite.co.uk)|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> The twenty-man teamsheets for two other pre-season friendlies against [[Warrington Wolves|Warrington]], and Widnes in 2008 also included Ainscough as a substitute, but he was never utilised in either game.<ref name="WWFapps">{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Player.AllMatches&o=217513|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Shaun Ainscough|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref>

The start of 2008 saw Ainscough being promoted from the academy sides to the reserve side, just one level below the first team. By coincidence, his first appearance and first try for the reserves was away against Leigh, as they had also been for his academy career, although this time, he played at fullback, not wing.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/wigan/?s=Matchstats&o=413347|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com: Leigh 12, Wigan 36|publisher=www.wiganwarriorsfans.com|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> The rest of the year saw five official appearances for Ainscough, scoring five tries including another hat-trick, this time against [[Wakefield Trinity]].<ref name="WWFapps" /> He also made four appearances in the Gillette Academy championship, scoring five tries.<ref name="WWFapps" />

His efforts in the reserves earned him a place in a pre-season friendly during the [[Christmas season]] of 2008 against Warrington, in which Ainscough picked up a try following a pass from [[Cameron Phelps]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warrington-v-Wigan.4820361.jp|title=Warriors ease past Wolves|publisher=www.wigantoday.net|accessdate=2009-06-30|last=Wilkinson|first=Phil|archive-date=16 April 2009|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090416173212/http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Warrington-v-Wigan.4820361.jp|url-status=dead}}</ref> It was his last match before being selected for the first team in 2009. In the whole of his career prior to starting first-class rugby league for Wigan, he appeared in 32 academy and reserve fixtures, of which 24 were won, with Ainscough personally scoring 28 tries.<ref name="WWFapps" />

===Wigan Warriors===
====Debut season====

Ainscough was promoted into Wigan first team on 22 January after receiving the number 28 jersey, which previously belonged to fellow academy product [[Sam Tomkins]] the season before.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/News_full.asp?newsid=4454|title=Warriors Announce 2009 Squad Numbers|publisher=www.wiganwarriors.com|accessdate=2009-06-30|archive-date=26 December 2024|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241226183625/https://www.wiganwarriors.com/News_full.asp?newsid=4454|url-status=dead}}</ref> His first Super League game was in a 22–28 home defeat by [[Castleford Tigers|Castleford]], a match in which he scored his first Super League try which looked to have given Wigan a chance of winning the match until Castleford scored another try through [[Michael Shenton]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7890283.stm|title=Wigan 22-28 Castleford|publisher=news.bbc.co.uk ([[BBC Sport]])|access-date=2009-06-30|date=2009-02-20}}</ref>
[[File:Shaun ainscough try for wigan vs barrow (05-04-09).JPG|thumb|right|upright=1.35|Ainscough dives for the line to score a try in the [[2009 Challenge Cup]] for [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] during their victory over [[Barrow Raiders|Barrow]]]]
However, Ainscough made a lasting contribution in his next appearance against Harlequins RL at the [[The Stoop|Twickenham Stoop]], in which he and [[Amos Roberts]] scored late in the match to give Wigan their first home win of the [[Super League XIV|2009 Super League season]].<ref>{{cite news
|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_league/7897632.stm|title=Harlequins 18-24 Wigan|publisher=news.bbc.co.uk|access-date=2009-06-30|date=2009-02-28}}</ref> Ainscough then featured in the Wigan team that defeated Bradford Bulls by 34 points at the [[JJB Stadium]],<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_facts/0,19935,11070_3115378,00.html |title=Wigan v Bradford - 7th March 2009 |publisher=www.skysports.com ([[Sky Sports]]) |accessdate=2009-06-30 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090429202012/http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_facts/0%2C19935%2C11070_3115378%2C00.html |archive-date=29 April 2009 |df=dmy-all }}</ref> before scoring a try on the way to a defeat at [[Leeds Rhinos|Leeds]] the week after.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_facts/0,19935,11070_3086276,00.html |title=Leeds v Wigan - 13th March 2009 |publisher=www.skysports.com |accessdate=2009-06-30 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090306030902/http://www.skysports.com/rugbyleague/match_facts/0%2C19935%2C11070_3086276%2C00.html |archive-date=6 March 2009 |df=dmy-all }}</ref>

An away victory over [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] in which he personally contributed two tries was to be the last win in the Super League for some weeks for Ainscough.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7928300.stm|title=Salford 12-38 Wigan|publisher=news.bbc.co.uk|access-date=2009-06-30|date=2009-03-20}}</ref> Although a home defeat by [[Huddersfield Giants]] was followed by a hat-trick performance at [[Barrow Raiders]] in the [[2009 Challenge Cup]] (a game in which Wigan were pushed hard by Barrow),<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/apr/05/barrow-raiders-wigan-warriors-challenge-cup|title=Challenge Cup: Barrow Raiders 20-32 Wigan Warriors|publisher=[[guardian.co.uk]]|accessdate=2009-06-30|last=Walker|first=Gareth|location=London|date=2009-04-05}}</ref> two further defeats at home to [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], and away to the [[Catalans Dragons]] in [[Perpignan]] forced Wigan coach [[Brian Noble (rugby league)|Brian Noble]] into changing the squad around, and even though Ainscough had contributed the only two tries of the night for Wigan against St Helens,<ref>
{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/Super_League_XIV_2009/Round_8/Wigan-vs-St_Helens/summary.html|title=Super League XIV 2009 - Round 8|publisher=www.rugbyleagueproject.org|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> he found himself out of the new squad, with Noble favouring [[Pat Richards]], and Amos Roberts on the [[Rugby league positions|wings]] with [[George Carmont]], and new Warrington-signing [[Martin Gleeson (rugby league)|Martin Gleeson]] at centre, and Cameron Phelps at fullback for Wigan's next game against [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]].<ref>
{{cite web|url=http://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/seasons/Super_League_XIV_2009/Round_10/Wigan-vs-Celtic/summary.html|title=Super League XIV 2009 - Round 10|publisher=www.rugbyleagueproject.org|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref>

The brief hiatus broke the very next game, however, as Ainscough (who had been omitted from the initial nineteen-man teamsheet) was called up late to replace a [[tonsillitis]]-stricken George Carmont. He personally ran-in four tries to secure a 26–40 victory against Wakefield Trinity at [[Belle Vue (Wakefield)|Belle Vue]], to the praise of commentators in the British mainstream national newspapers, and drawing comparisons to the Wigan legend, [[Martin Offiah]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/sport/rugby_league/article6175390.ece|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090508071128/http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/sport/rugby_league/article6175390.ece|url-status=dead|archive-date=8 May 2009|title=Shaun Ainscough celebrates late call-up in style|publisher=www.timesonline.co.uk (The Times)|access-date=2009-06-30|last=Irvine|first= Christopher|location=London|date=2009-04-27}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby/2009/04/27/4-try-shaun-blitzes-the-wildcats-115875-21311702|title=Wakefield 26-40 Wigan: 4-try Shaun Ainscough blitzes Wildcats|publisher=www.mirror.co.uk ([[Daily Mirror|The Mirror]])|accessdate=2009-06-30|last=Baxtor|first=Trever}}
</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2009/apr/26/wakefield-trinity-wigan-warriors-shaun-ainscough-super-league|title=Super League: Wakefield Trinity 26-40 Wigan Warriors: Teenager Shaun Ainscough impresses with four tries|publisher=guardian.co.uk|accessdate=2009-06-30|last=Wilson|first=Andy|location=London|date=2009-04-26}}</ref><ref name="Telegraph">{{cite news|url=https://www.telegraph.co.uk/sport/rugbyleague/5226440/Formidable-Shaun-Ainscough-guides-Wigan-to-victory-over-Wakefield.html|title=Formidable Shaun Ainscough guides Wigan to victory over Wakefield|publisher=telegraph.co.uk ([[Daily Telegraph]])|accessdate=2009-06-30|location=London|date=2009-04-26}}</ref> The match brought his Super League tally of tries for the season to thirteen, the most of anyone in the league by that point. Despite this, Ainscough has only appeared in two more matches to date – an away defeat by [[Warrington Wolves]], and another away defeat by [[North Wales Crusaders|Celtic Crusaders]].

His capabilities in defence drew criticism from some coaches, not least his own coach at Wigan. Following the game at [[Wakefield Trinity]], Noble stated that, "there are other parts of his game he needs to improve".<ref name="Telegraph" /> In a pre-match interview before the [[Magic Weekend]] in Round 12, St Helens coach [[Mick Potter]] stated he was a target for high kicks, with the belief that Ainscough's opposite winger [[Ade Gardner]], the leading try scorer in 2008's [[Super League XIII]], would put him under pressure.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.liverpoolecho.co.uk/sport/st-helens/2009/04/30/wigan-wonder-teen-is-warned-100252-23510728|title=Wigan wonder teen is warned
|publisher=www.liverpoolecho.co.uk|accessdate=2009-06-30}}</ref> It was a game Ainscough did not eventually play in.

After losing his place, Ainscough continued to play in the reserves, although in August it was confirmed he had suffered [[Scaphoid fracture|scaphoid wrist damage]], and as a result would possibly be out for the rest of the 2009 season.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_5489484,00.html|title=Ainscough to miss run-in|publisher=[[Sky Sports]]|accessdate=14 August 2009|date=13 August 2009}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/8200776.stm|title=Injured Ainscough out for season|publisher=[[BBC News]]|access-date=14 August 2009|date=13 August 2009}}</ref>

====2010 season====
Ainscough recovered from injury, and in December 2009, he was confirmed as a player in Wigan's first team for the 2010 season.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.wiganwarriors.com/news_full.asp?newsid=5337|title=Warriors Announce 2010 Squad Numbers|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=8 December 2009|accessdate=5 February 2010|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100123093324/http://www.wiganwarriors.com/news_full.asp?newsid=5337|archive-date=23 January 2010 }}</ref> Before the 2010 season started, Wigan signed a dual registration agreement with [[Co-operative Championship]] side Widnes for Ainscough and his team-mate, [[Liam Farrell]]. The agreement allowed Ainscough to play for Widnes whenever a place in Wigan's seventeen-man squad was unavailable, with the exception of [[Challenge Cup]] matches. Ainscough was recalled from Widnes and has since joined [[Castleford Tigers]] on an initial one-month loan.<ref name="Widnes">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/wigan/8500043.stm|title=Wigan Warriors trio sign up with Widnes Vikings|publisher=[[British Broadcasting Corporation|BBC]]|date=5 February 2010|access-date=5 February 2010}}</ref> Shaun has now agreed to stay at Castleford for the whole of the 2010 season.<ref name="Castleford RLFC A to Z Player List (All Time)">{{cite web|url=http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/alltime.php|title=Castleford RLFC A to Z Player List (All Time)|publisher=thecastlefordtigers.co.uk |date=31 December 2014|accessdate=1 January 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120216084010/http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/alltime.php|archive-date=16 February 2012 }}</ref><ref name="Statistics at thecastlefordtigers.co.uk">{{cite web|url=http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/playerprofile.php?pid=1042|title=Statistics at thecastlefordtigers.co.uk|publisher=thecastlefordtigers.co.uk |date=31 December 2014|accessdate=1 January 2015|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120216084416/http://www.thecastlefordtigers.co.uk/playerprofile.php?pid=1042|archive-date=16 February 2012 }}</ref>

===Bradford Bulls===
Ainscough signed a one-year contract at the end of the 2010 season to play for [[Bradford Bulls]] in 2011. He appeared in two of the four pre-season games, firstly against [[Halifax R.L.F.C.|Halifax]], and then Wakefield Trinity. In the regular season, he featured in eight consecutive games from round 3 Crusaders to round 10 [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]]. His next appearance was in round 13 Warrington. He recovered from surgery to play in Round 26 Crusaders and round 27 Wakefield Trinity.

Ainscough appeared in three of the four pre-season friendlies, he played against [[Castleford Tigers]], [[Dewsbury Rams|Dewsbury]] and [[Keighley Cougars|Keighley]]. He scored a try against Castleford and another against Dewsbury.

Ainscough featured in five consecutive games from round 10 Leeds to round 14 Salford. He featured in eleven consecutive games from round 16 Castleford to round 26 Hull F.C.. He also featured in the [[Challenge Cup]] against [[Doncaster R.L.F.C.|Doncaster]] and Warrington.

After the 2012 season he chose not to accept a new contract with Bradford which offered him less money than the previous two years. This lead him to make the decision to move to the South City Bulls in [[New South Wales]], Australia.

===Batley===
Between 2015 and 2018, Ainscough played for Batley making over 80 appearances. On 2 September 2015, Ainscough famously kicked the ball across the field in an attempt to find the touchline in Batley's game against Featherstone. Batley were winning the match 26–22 after the final siren when Ainscough attempted the kick. The ball did not reach the touchline and it was picked up by a Featherstone player who scored a try to make it 26-26. [[Paul Sykes (rugby league)|Paul Sykes]] would then kick a goal from the side line to win the match for Featherstone 28–26.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.smh.com.au/sport/nrl/batley-bulldogs-player-kicks-for-touch-misses-and-loses-game-after-gifting-a-try-20150902-gjd5wf.html/|title=Batley Bulldogs player kicks for touch, misses and loses game after gifting a try|website=www.smh.com.au}}</ref>

===South City Bulls===
After Ainscough finished at Bradford Bulls he moved to Australia to play part-time for the South City Bulls whilst also taking on a regular job.

===Rochdale Hornets===
After joining for the 2019 season, it was announced in July 2020 that Ainscough had committed to stay at [[Rochdale Hornets|Rochdale]]<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.loverugbyleague.com/post/shaun-ainscough-commits-future-to-rochdale/|title=Shaun Ainscough commits future to Rochdale|website=www.loverugbyleague.com|date=9 Jul 2020|accessdate=21 Dec 2020}}</ref>
